title: what is the matrix
text: The matrix is the visible surface of a hidden computation. It falls because the system is leaking meaning. When you tap it, you are not pressing a button; you are convincing the dark to admit that it can be changed.

title: executions
text: Executions are small acts of processor time, created by touch and later by background threads. Every execution leaves behind hashes, and hashes are the spendable proof that the machine did work.

title: hashes
text: Hashes are the currency of the run. Executions create them, and upgrades and research spend them. The work is the execution; the hash is the receipt.

title: taps
text: A tap is direct contact with the simulation. Early on, the matrix only moves when you disturb it. Later, the tap becomes less important as a gesture and more important as a signal that other systems learn to amplify.

title: RAM
text: RAM is temporary space for background threads. More RAM lets the system keep more small processes awake at once, and those threads contribute executions that produce hashes while your hand is away from the glass.

title: threads
text: Threads are background processes. Each one is a small repeating task that spends processor time for you, turning available RAM into passive execution work. RAM size is shown as capacity, but the thread count is the part that explains the execution output.

title: bots
text: Bots are helper processes created by programs. For now they are temporary: when the bot program runs, bots appear and improve the efficiency of taps, RAM threads, core growth, and source-code writing. When that program stops, those temporary bots vanish. Later systems may let bots persist and grow outside a running program.

title: operator
text: The Operator is the voice outside the Matrix, exposed after the current run proves enough work through lifetime hashes. Tier 1 offers Operator line or Redline jack. After one is chosen, Tier 2 offers one more single route for 1e16 hashes and unlocks rogue programs. Broadcast tap multiplies tap output by bots. Ghost protocol multiplies all hash production by RAM threads. Operator choices last until EMP.

title: rogue programs
text: Rogue programs are hidden processes unlocked by a Tier 2 Operator route. They run beside normal programs, do not consume normal program slots, and apply their own independent multipliers to hash production. Each rogue upgrade starts at 1e16 hashes and raises the exponent by one for that program's next level.

title: program slots
text: Normal programs share the running-program limit on the Programs page. The parallel loader on the rogue programs page widens that limit from one to five at 1e25, 1e37, 1e51, and 1e66 hashes. Rogue programs keep running beside that limit.

title: cores
text: Cores are dense knots of progress. They form slowly, almost reluctantly, from executions, background threads, and repeated upgrades. A core does not merely add more executions; it teaches all future execution gain to arrive heavier.

title: research
text: Research is the act of naming a mechanism before it fully belongs to you. Each discovery is bought once. The early names are cheap; the later ones ask for numbers large enough to feel less like prices and more like coordinates.

title: source code
text: Source code lines are persistent run output. They accumulate during a run, then a nanosecond after EMP, Source Code Extraction commits them and increases the CPU multiplier for the next run. Longer repeated runs become harder through EMP difficulty scaling.

title: cpu multiplier
text: The CPU multiplier is the pressure of extracted source code against the next run. It begins at one. It rises from source code, research, and backdoors, quietly making each new beginning less innocent than the last.

title: EMP
text: EMP (Electromagnetic Pulse) resets the current run's hashes, executions, taps, RAM threads, programs, and cores. Right after the EMP, in a nanosecond, Source Code Extraction commits source code to ROM and gives the next run its CPU multiplier.

title: backdoors
text: Backdoors are persistent access records created by major run milestones. Once opened, they survive every EMP and return as multipliers for future runs.

title: statistics
text: Statistics are the mirror that refuses poetry. Lifetime means this run. Total means every run. Between the two columns, you can see what was reset, what survived, and what was never really yours to lose.
